questions.examTip: Glue Data Quality for declarative validation rules. Lambda for real-time custom logic.

questions.scenario: A call center wants to transcribe recorded customer calls and identify which agent and which customer is speaking throughout the call.
questions.question: Which Amazon Transcribe feature identifies different speakers in an audio recording?

options.text: Custom vocabularies
Content redaction
Speaker diarization
Real-time streaming

questions.explanation: Speaker diarization identifies different speakers in audio—who said what. This is essential for call center analytics where you need to distinguish between agent and customer speech.

incorrectExplanations.a: Custom vocabularies improve accuracy for domain-specific terms, not speaker identification.
incorrectExplanations.b: Content redaction removes sensitive information, not speaker identification.
incorrectExplanations.d: Real-time streaming is for live transcription, not speaker identification.

questions.articleReference: Data Pipelines > Transcribe: 'Speaker diarization: Identifies different speakers (who said what)'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Who said what = speaker diarization.

questions.examTip: Speaker diarization = identifies who said what in audio.

questions.scenario: A developer is building a RAG application. User inputs sometimes contain typos, abbreviations, and lack context. They want to improve AI response quality without changing the model.
questions.question: What preprocessing technique would MOST improve response quality for the described input issues?

options.text: Base64 encoding
Token counting
JSON minification
Text normalization and context injection

questions.explanation: Text normalization cleans up input (expanding abbreviations, fixing typos, standardizing formats), and context injection adds background information (user history, resolved references). Together they transform messy input into clean, contextualized queries.

incorrectExplanations.a: Base64 encoding is for binary data transmission, not text quality improvement.
incorrectExplanations.b: Token counting measures input size but doesn't improve quality.
incorrectExplanations.c: JSON minification reduces size but doesn't improve content quality.

questions.articleReference: Data Pipelines > Input Enhancement: 'Text Normalization: Expand 'u' to 'you', fix typos, standardize formats. Context Enrichment: Inject background, resolve references.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Messy input = normalize + enrich context.

questions.examTip: Input enhancement: normalize text + inject context. Improves AI output without model changes.

questions.scenario: A multimodal AI application accepts images from users. Some images are corrupted, some are in unsupported formats, and some exceed size limits. The team needs robust error handling.
questions.question: What is the recommended approach for handling corrupt or invalid image inputs in a production pipeline?

options.text: Crash the pipeline and alert operators immediately
Send all images regardless of validity and let the model handle errors
Skip bad inputs, log issues, and continue processing remaining images
Attempt to repair all corrupted images automatically

questions.explanation: Handle errors gracefully: skip bad inputs, log the issues for investigation, and continue processing valid images. Don't let one bad file crash the entire pipeline. The model won't tell you when input is bad—it'll produce garbage output.

incorrectExplanations.a: Crashing on every bad input is too aggressive for batch processing.
incorrectExplanations.b: Sending invalid images wastes resources and produces garbage output.
incorrectExplanations.d: Automatic repair is unreliable for corrupted files.

questions.articleReference: Data Pipelines > Error Handling: 'Corrupted images, garbled audio, password-protected PDFs—they all happen. Skip bad inputs, log issues, continue processing.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Bad inputs in batch = skip, log, continue. Don't crash or ignore.

questions.examTip: Graceful error handling: skip bad inputs, log issues, continue processing.

questions.scenario: A developer constructs JSON payloads for Bedrock using string concatenation instead of serialization libraries. Their requests sometimes fail with parse errors.
questions.question: What is the likely cause of the parse errors and the recommended fix?

options.text: Special characters not properly escaped; use proper JSON serialization libraries
Wrong model ID; verify the model identifier
Token limits exceeded; reduce input length
Network timeouts; increase connection timeout

questions.explanation: Newlines, quotes, backslashes, and control characters break JSON if not escaped correctly. String concatenation makes these escaping errors common. Use proper serialization libraries (json.dumps, JSON.stringify) instead of manual concatenation.

incorrectExplanations.b: Wrong model ID causes authorization errors, not parse errors.
incorrectExplanations.c: Token limits cause different errors, not parse errors.
incorrectExplanations.d: Network issues cause timeout errors, not parse errors.

questions.articleReference: Data Pipelines > Special Characters: 'Need proper escaping in JSON. Newlines, quotes, backslashes, control characters—they'll all break your JSON if not escaped correctly. Use proper serialization libraries, not string concatenation.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: JSON parse errors = escaping issues. Use serialization libraries.

questions.examTip: Always use JSON serialization libraries. String concatenation causes escaping errors.

questions.scenario: A team is processing expense reports. They need to extract vendor names, amounts, dates, and line items from receipt images.
questions.question: Which Amazon Textract API is optimized for this use case?

options.text: AnalyzeID
AnalyzeExpense
AnalyzeDocument
DetectDocumentText

questions.explanation: AnalyzeExpense is specifically optimized for receipts and invoices. It extracts vendor information, amounts, dates, and line items with higher accuracy than generic document analysis for this document type.

incorrectExplanations.a: AnalyzeID is for identity documents, not expense documents.
incorrectExplanations.c: AnalyzeDocument works for general forms but AnalyzeExpense is optimized specifically for receipts.
incorrectExplanations.d: DetectDocumentText only does basic OCR—no semantic understanding of expense documents.

questions.articleReference: Data Pipelines > Textract: 'AnalyzeExpense: Receipts and invoices'

eliminationHints: Match Textract API to document type
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Receipts/invoices = AnalyzeExpense. Forms = AnalyzeDocument. IDs = AnalyzeID.

questions.examTip: AnalyzeExpense for receipts and invoices. Know Textract API specializations.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application's JSON formatting logic is scattered across ten different code files. A Bedrock API change now requires updates to the payload structure.
questions.question: What architectural pattern would have prevented this maintenance burden?

options.text: Centralize formatting logic in a shared utility layer
Use API Gateway for all Bedrock calls
Use a microservices architecture
Cache all responses to avoid frequent API calls

questions.explanation: Build a formatting layer—don't construct JSON throughout your codebase. Create utility functions that handle serialization consistently. When the API changes, you fix one place instead of ten.

incorrectExplanations.b: API Gateway doesn't handle payload construction.
incorrectExplanations.c: Microservices don't solve the formatting duplication problem.
incorrectExplanations.d: Caching doesn't address the formatting maintenance issue.

questions.articleReference: Data Pipelines > Best Practice: 'Build a formatting layer. Don't construct JSON throughout your codebase. When the API changes, you fix one place.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Formatting scattered = centralize in utility layer.

questions.examTip: Centralize formatting logic. One place to fix when APIs change.

questions.scenario: A team is building their first RAG application. They want minimal operational overhead and automatic handling of document parsing, chunking, embedding generation, and storage.
questions.question: Which vector store option provides the SIMPLEST path to production RAG?

options.text: Custom FAISS index on EC2
Self-managed OpenSearch cluster
Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector extension
Bedrock Knowledge Bases with managed vector store

questions.explanation: Bedrock Knowledge Bases handles everything: parsing PDFs and Word docs, chunking them into pieces, generating embeddings, storing vectors, and keeping everything synced when documents change. Most RAG projects should start here.

incorrectExplanations.a: Custom FAISS requires significant implementation work and operational management.
incorrectExplanations.b: Self-managed OpenSearch requires operational expertise and doesn't handle the full RAG pipeline.
incorrectExplanations.c: Aurora pgvector requires you to implement parsing, chunking, and embedding generation yourself.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Bedrock Knowledge Bases: 'Does everything you need, zero maintenance. Most RAG projects should start here.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Simplest RAG = Bedrock Knowledge Bases.

questions.examTip: Bedrock KB is the starting point for RAG. Handles parsing, chunking, embedding, storage.

questions.scenario: A company has deployed a RAG system with pure vector search. Users searching for error code 'E-1042' get results about 'errors' generally instead of the specific error code documentation.
questions.question: What search mode would fix this issue?

options.text: Enable HYBRID search mode to combine keyword matching with vector similarity
Increase the number of retrieved documents (higher k)
Use larger embedding dimensions
Switch to a different embedding model

questions.explanation: Pure semantic search might return docs about 'errors' generally when searching for 'E-1042'. Hybrid search combines vector similarity with keyword matching (BM25), catching exact term matches that pure semantic search misses.

incorrectExplanations.b: Retrieving more documents doesn't fix the fundamental semantic vs. keyword matching issue.
incorrectExplanations.c: Larger dimensions don't improve exact term matching.
incorrectExplanations.d: Different embedding models still use semantic similarity, not keyword matching.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Common Mistakes: 'Vector search only: When users search for exact terms like error code E-1042, pure semantic search fails. Enable hybrid mode.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Exact term matching failing = enable hybrid search.

questions.examTip: Hybrid search for exact terms. Pure vector search misses keyword matches.

questions.scenario: A team is ingesting technical manuals into their RAG system. The manuals have nested structure with chapters, sections, and subsections. Users ask questions that require understanding both specific details and broader chapter context.
questions.question: Which chunking strategy is RECOMMENDED for this structured content?

options.text: Semantic chunking at paragraph boundaries
Sentence-level chunking for maximum precision
Fixed-size chunking with 512 tokens
Hierarchical chunking with parent and child chunks

questions.explanation: Hierarchical chunking creates parent chunks (~1000 tokens) and child chunks (~500 tokens) with overlap. During retrieval, find relevant children but can substitute broader parent chunks for context. Best for technical manuals, legal documents, anything with nested structure.

incorrectExplanations.a: Semantic chunking is good for uniformly dense prose but doesn't preserve hierarchical relationships.
incorrectExplanations.b: Sentence-level is too granular and loses section-level context.
incorrectExplanations.c: Fixed-size chunking ignores document structure and may split content mid-section.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Hierarchical Chunking: 'Creates parent chunks (~1000 tokens) and child chunks (~500 tokens). Best for: Technical manuals, legal documents, anything with nested structure.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Structured documents with hierarchy = hierarchical chunking.

questions.examTip: Hierarchical chunking for structured docs. Parent chunks for context, children for details.

questions.scenario: A team deployed a RAG system and later realized they need metadata filtering by department and access level. They didn't plan metadata fields before bulk ingestion.
questions.question: What is the consequence of not planning metadata schema before bulk ingestion?

options.text: The system will automatically infer metadata from content
Only new documents will have metadata, old ones work without it
Metadata can be added retroactively with no impact
Adding filter fields requires re-processing all documents

questions.explanation: Adding metadata fields after ingestion typically means re-processing all documents. You cannot change chunking strategy after creating a data source. Plan your metadata schema before bulk ingestion to avoid this costly re-work.

incorrectExplanations.a: Metadata must be explicitly defined—it's not auto-inferred.
incorrectExplanations.b: Having inconsistent metadata creates filtering problems.
incorrectExplanations.c: Adding metadata retroactively requires re-ingestion of all documents.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Plan Your Schema: 'Design metadata fields before bulk ingestion. Adding fields later typically means re-processing all documents.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Metadata changes after ingestion = re-process everything.

questions.examTip: Plan metadata schema BEFORE bulk ingestion. Changes require re-processing.

questions.scenario: A team already uses PostgreSQL extensively and wants to add vector search to their existing database without introducing a new technology stack.
questions.question: Which vector store option integrates with their existing PostgreSQL infrastructure?

options.text: Amazon DynamoDB
Amazon OpenSearch with k-NN plugin
Amazon MemoryDB for Redis
Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector extension

questions.explanation: Aurora pgvector adds vector operations to PostgreSQL. If your team already uses PostgreSQL, querying vectors with familiar SQL syntax is simpler than introducing a new database. You can join vector results with relational data.

incorrectExplanations.a: DynamoDB is for metadata, not vector similarity search—it lacks vector capabilities.
incorrectExplanations.b: OpenSearch is a separate technology stack, not PostgreSQL-based.
incorrectExplanations.c: MemoryDB for Redis is an in-memory store, not PostgreSQL.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Aurora pgvector: 'If your team already uses PostgreSQL, adding vectors to the same database simplifies your architecture. Query vectors with familiar SQL syntax.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Already using PostgreSQL = Aurora pgvector.

questions.examTip: Aurora pgvector for existing PostgreSQL teams. Query vectors with SQL.

questions.scenario: A vector search is taking seconds instead of milliseconds for a database with millions of vectors.
questions.question: Which indexing algorithm provides fast approximate nearest neighbor search at scale?

options.text: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs)
B-tree index
Hash index
Linear scan (brute force)

questions.explanation: HNSW creates a layered graph structure that finds approximate nearest neighbors incredibly fast. It might not find mathematically perfect matches, but it finds very close matches in milliseconds instead of seconds. That trade-off is worth it.

incorrectExplanations.b: B-tree is for ordered data, not vector similarity search.
incorrectExplanations.c: Hash index is for exact matches, not similarity search.
incorrectExplanations.d: Linear scan is O(n) and doesn't scale to millions of vectors.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > HNSW: 'HNSW creates a layered graph structure that finds approximate nearest neighbors incredibly fast... finds very close matches in milliseconds instead of seconds.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Fast vector search at scale = HNSW indexes.

questions.examTip: HNSW for fast approximate nearest neighbor search. Trade perfect accuracy for speed.

questions.scenario: A RAG system needs to ensure users only retrieve documents they're authorized to access. User A should not see documents tagged as confidential to User B's department.
questions.question: How should access control be implemented in the vector store?

options.text: Filter results in application code after retrieval
Create separate vector stores for each user
Rely on document encryption to prevent unauthorized access
Use metadata pre-filtering based on user's access level before vector search

questions.explanation: Tag documents with access levels in metadata, then pre-filter based on who's asking before vector search. Pre-filtering is efficient because you're searching a smaller pile, and users can't retrieve documents they shouldn't see—even if those docs are perfect semantic matches.

incorrectExplanations.a: Post-filtering risks returning too few results if many matches fail the filter.
incorrectExplanations.b: Separate vector stores per user doesn't scale and creates management overhead.
incorrectExplanations.c: Encryption prevents reading but doesn't prevent retrieval of unauthorized documents.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Access Control: 'Tag documents with access levels, then filter based on who's asking. Users can't retrieve documents they shouldn't see—even if those docs are perfect semantic matches.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Access control in RAG = metadata filtering before search.

questions.examTip: Access control = metadata pre-filtering. Search smaller pile, prevent unauthorized retrieval.

questions.scenario: A team chose OpenSearch for their RAG system 'just in case' they need advanced features. Six months later, they're spending significant time on cluster management and tuning, but haven't used any OpenSearch-specific features.
questions.question: According to vector store selection best practices, what mistake did they make?

options.text: They over-engineered by choosing OpenSearch when Bedrock KB would have sufficed
They should have chosen Aurora pgvector instead
They needed a larger instance size
They should have added more shards to improve performance

questions.explanation: Choosing OpenSearch 'just in case' when Bedrock KB would be plenty is like buying a sports car for grocery runs—you're paying for complexity you don't need. Start with Bedrock KB; move to OpenSearch only when you genuinely need its superpowers.

incorrectExplanations.b: Aurora pgvector is appropriate for PostgreSQL teams, but the issue is over-engineering.
incorrectExplanations.c: Instance size isn't the issue—they chose the wrong service entirely.
incorrectExplanations.d: More shards don't address the fundamental over-engineering problem.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Common Mistakes: 'Choosing OpenSearch "just in case" when Bedrock KB would be plenty. Like buying a sports car for grocery runs—you're paying for complexity you don't need.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Don't choose complex solutions 'just in case'. Start with Bedrock KB.

questions.examTip: Don't over-engineer. Bedrock KB first, OpenSearch when you need its features.

questions.scenario: A company's Bedrock Knowledge Base is configured with fixed-size chunking at 512 tokens. They want to change to hierarchical chunking for better context preservation.
questions.question: Can they change the chunking strategy for the existing data source?

options.text: Yes, but only during scheduled maintenance windows
Yes, by using the UpdateDataSource API
Yes, update the configuration and re-sync
No, you cannot change chunking strategy after creating a data source—must recreate

questions.explanation: You cannot change chunking strategy after creating a data source. Choose wisely upfront, or you'll have to delete the data source, create a new one with the desired chunking, and re-ingest everything.

incorrectExplanations.a: Maintenance windows don't enable chunking changes.
incorrectExplanations.b: UpdateDataSource doesn't allow changing chunking strategy.
incorrectExplanations.c: Chunking strategy is fixed at data source creation time.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Critical Point: 'You cannot change chunking strategy after creating a data source. Choose wisely upfront, or you'll re-ingest everything.'

eliminationHints: Chunking is permanent per data source
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Chunking strategy = fixed at creation. Must recreate to change.

questions.examTip: Chunking strategy cannot be changed. Choose correctly before creating data source.

questions.scenario: A team is using DynamoDB to store their RAG document vectors and perform similarity searches.
questions.question: What is wrong with this approach?

options.text: DynamoDB doesn't support vector similarity search—it's for metadata, not vectors
DynamoDB requires provisioned capacity for vectors
DynamoDB vectors are limited to 256 dimensions
DynamoDB is too expensive for this use case

questions.explanation: DynamoDB is for metadata storage, not vector similarity search. It lacks the k-NN capabilities needed for semantic search. Use a proper vector store like Bedrock KB, OpenSearch, or Aurora pgvector for vector search.

incorrectExplanations.b: This isn't relevant because DynamoDB doesn't support vectors.
incorrectExplanations.c: DynamoDB doesn't have vector dimensions because it doesn't support vectors.
incorrectExplanations.d: Cost isn't the primary issue—functionality is.

questions.articleReference: Vector Stores > Common Mistakes: 'DynamoDB for vectors: DynamoDB is for metadata, not vector similarity search. Use a proper vector store.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Vector search requires proper vector DB. DynamoDB is for metadata.

questions.examTip: DynamoDB is for metadata, NOT vector similarity search.

questions.scenario: A RAG system retrieves the top 5 chunks, but 4 of them come from the same document and say essentially the same thing. Users complain about repetitive, unhelpful responses.
questions.question: Which retrieval technique addresses this redundancy problem?

options.text: Increase retrieval to top 10 chunks
Use MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance) to penalize redundancy
Switch to keyword-only search
Use a larger chunk size

questions.explanation: MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance) solves the redundancy problem. It penalizes results that are too similar to already-selected results, selecting chunks that are both relevant AND diverse.

incorrectExplanations.a: Retrieving more chunks may return even more redundant results.
incorrectExplanations.c: Keyword search doesn't inherently solve redundancy.
incorrectExplanations.d: Larger chunks don't address the redundancy between chunks.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > MMR: 'Solves the redundancy problem. Without it, you might get five chunks from the same document all saying the same thing. MMR penalizes redundancy, selecting results that are both relevant AND diverse.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Redundant results = use MMR for diversity.

questions.examTip: MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance) = relevance + diversity. Reduces redundancy.

questions.scenario: A team wants to improve retrieval precision for high-stakes queries. They're willing to accept higher latency for better accuracy. Currently they retrieve top 5 results directly.
questions.question: What retrieval pattern should they implement?

options.text: Retrieve broadly (top 20), then rerank to top 5 using the Rerank API
Add more documents to the knowledge base
Use larger embedding dimensions
Retrieve top 5 with higher similarity threshold

questions.explanation: The pattern is: retrieve broad, rerank narrow. Get top-20 candidates with fast vector search, then rerank to top-5 with a cross-encoder that jointly processes query and document. Cross-encoders catch relevance signals that separate embeddings miss.

incorrectExplanations.b: More documents doesn't improve precision of what's retrieved.
incorrectExplanations.c: Larger dimensions don't improve ranking accuracy as much as reranking.
incorrectExplanations.d: Higher threshold might miss relevant documents; doesn't improve ranking quality.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Reranking: 'Retrieve wide, rerank narrow. Vector search grabs top-N candidates (say, 20). Reranker scores each query-document pair. Return top-K (say, 5).'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Precision-critical = retrieve broadly, rerank narrowly.

questions.examTip: Reranking: retrieve 20, rerank to 5. Cross-encoders see query+doc together.

questions.scenario: A user asks: 'Compare the cost and performance of EC2 vs Lambda for batch processing.' The RAG system returns scattered, partial information.
questions.question: Which query handling technique would help retrieve more complete information?

options.text: Query decomposition into sub-queries
Query truncation to reduce complexity
Query caching for faster response
Query expansion with synonyms

questions.explanation: This complex question is really four questions: EC2 cost, Lambda cost, EC2 performance, Lambda performance. Query decomposition breaks it apart, retrieves for each sub-query, then combines results. The FM gets all relevant context instead of a muddled mix.

incorrectExplanations.b: Truncation would lose important parts of the query.
incorrectExplanations.c: Caching doesn't improve retrieval quality.
incorrectExplanations.d: Synonyms don't help with multi-part questions.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Query Decomposition: 'Handle complex multi-part questions. "Compare cost and performance of EC2 vs Lambda" is really four questions. Break it apart, retrieve for each sub-query, combine results.'

eliminationHints: Compare X vs Y = multiple sub-queries
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Complex comparison questions = query decomposition.

questions.examTip: Query decomposition for multi-part questions. Break apart, retrieve separately, combine.

questions.scenario: A RAG system struggles with terse queries that don't match document vocabulary well. For example, 'AWS compute options' doesn't retrieve documents about Lambda, EC2, or Fargate.
questions.question: Which technique generates better retrieval queries from terse user input?

options.text: Reduce retrieval count
Use a smaller embedding model
Increase chunk overlap
HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings)—generate a hypothetical answer, then embed that

questions.explanation: HyDE asks an FM to write a hypothetical answer document, then embeds THAT for retrieval. The hypothetical document is written in 'document style,' matching how actual documents are written much better than a terse query.

incorrectExplanations.a: Fewer results doesn't improve query quality.
incorrectExplanations.b: Smaller models don't solve vocabulary mismatch.
incorrectExplanations.c: Chunk overlap doesn't help with query-document vocabulary mismatch.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > HyDE: 'Ask an FM to write a hypothetical answer document. Embed THAT for retrieval. The hypothetical document is written in "document style," matching actual documents much better than a terse query.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Terse queries missing matches = HyDE for better query embeddings.

questions.examTip: HyDE: generate hypothetical answer, embed that. Better matching for terse queries.

questions.scenario: A team indexed their documents with Titan Embeddings V1. They want to switch to Cohere Embed for better multilingual support. Can they just update the query to use Cohere?
questions.question: What happens if they query with Cohere embeddings against a Titan-indexed vector store?

options.text: Results will be slightly less accurate but functional
The system will automatically translate between embedding spaces
Only multilingual queries will fail
Retrieval will completely fail—different models produce incompatible vector spaces

questions.explanation: All documents and all queries must use the same embedding model. Different models produce completely different vector spaces—a Titan embedding and a Cohere embedding for the same text won't be close to each other. Switching models means re-embedding everything.

incorrectExplanations.a: It won't be slightly less accurate—it will be completely broken.
incorrectExplanations.b: There's no automatic translation between embedding spaces.
incorrectExplanations.c: All queries will fail, not just multilingual ones.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > The Critical Rule: 'All documents and all queries must use the same embedding model. You can't embed docs with Titan and query with Cohere—the vector spaces are completely different.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Query and document embeddings MUST use same model.

questions.examTip: Same embedding model for indexing and querying. Different models = incompatible spaces.

questions.scenario: A team is choosing an embedding model for a system that will process documents in English, Spanish, French, and German.
questions.question: Which embedding model is recommended for this multilingual use case?

options.text: Cohere Embed Multilingual
Amazon Titan Embeddings V1
Use separate models per language
Any model works equally well for multilingual

questions.explanation: Cohere Embed (available through Bedrock) shines for multilingual content and often outperforms Titan for documents spanning multiple languages. However, it costs ~5x more.

incorrectExplanations.b: Titan V1 has limited multilingual support compared to Cohere.
incorrectExplanations.c: Separate models per language creates complexity and cross-language retrieval issues.
incorrectExplanations.d: Models have different multilingual capabilities—not all work equally well.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Cohere Embed: 'Available through Bedrock, shines for multilingual content. Often outperforms Titan for documents spanning multiple languages. Costs ~5x more.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Multilingual documents = Cohere Embed.

questions.examTip: Cohere Embed for multilingual. Costs 5x more but better cross-language performance.

questions.scenario: A chunk size of 2000 tokens is producing poor retrieval results. The embeddings seem to capture vague 'everything in this section' rather than specific concepts.
questions.question: What is the likely cause and recommended fix?

options.text: Enable hybrid search
Chunks are too small; increase to 4000 tokens
Chunks are too large; embeddings become 'semantic mush' and should be reduced to 300-500 tokens
Use a different embedding model

questions.explanation: Large chunks (2000+ tokens) produce semantic mush—a vague average that doesn't match specific queries well. The article recommends 300-500 tokens as a balanced default. Smaller chunks match queries more precisely.

incorrectExplanations.a: Hybrid search helps with exact matches but doesn't fix the chunking problem.
incorrectExplanations.b: Larger chunks would make the semantic mush problem worse.
incorrectExplanations.d: The model isn't the issue—the chunk size is.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Chunk Size: 'A 2000-token chunk about "everything in Chapter 5" won't match as well as a focused chunk. 300-500 tokens: Balanced default for most cases.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Vague retrieval = chunks too large. Reduce to 300-500 tokens.

questions.examTip: 300-500 tokens is the balanced chunk size. Too large = semantic mush.

questions.scenario: The default Bedrock KB chunking parameters are causing some answers to be split across chunk boundaries, resulting in incomplete retrieval.
questions.question: What parameter should be adjusted to ensure related content appears in multiple chunks?

options.text: Use sentence-level chunking
Increase chunk size to 1000 tokens
Add chunk overlap (10-20% of chunk size)
Disable chunking entirely

questions.explanation: Overlap prevents losing context at boundaries. With 10-20% overlap, adjacent chunks share content. When an answer spans a boundary, both chunks contain it, improving recall.

incorrectExplanations.a: Sentence-level is too granular and loses context.
incorrectExplanations.b: Larger chunks might help but don't guarantee boundary coverage.
incorrectExplanations.d: Disabling chunking isn't possible for large documents.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Overlapping Chunks: 'Add redundancy at boundaries. If chunks are 512 tokens with 50-token overlap, adjacent chunks share content. Typical overlap: 10-20% of chunk size.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Boundary issues = add chunk overlap.

questions.examTip: Chunk overlap (10-20%) prevents losing context at boundaries.

questions.scenario: An FM needs to retrieve information from a knowledge base only when needed, not for every query. Sometimes it can answer from its training data; other times it needs external documents.
questions.question: How should the retrieval be integrated with the FM?

options.text: Pre-fetch all documents and include in system prompt
Let users manually trigger retrieval
Use function calling—define a retrieval tool the FM invokes when it needs external information
Always retrieve and include context for every query

questions.explanation: Function calling lets FMs invoke retrieval tools only when needed. Define a retrieval function with parameters (query, filters, top_k), and the FM calls it when it needs external information. More flexible than always-retrieve approaches.

incorrectExplanations.a: Pre-fetching everything is impractical and hits token limits.
incorrectExplanations.b: Manual triggering is poor UX and defeats the purpose of intelligent retrieval.
incorrectExplanations.d: Always retrieving adds latency and cost for queries that don't need it.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Function Calling: 'Let FMs invoke retrieval tools. Define a retrieval function with parameters, and the FM calls it when it needs external information. More flexible than always-retrieve approaches.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Conditional retrieval = function calling/tools.

questions.examTip: Function calling lets FM decide when to retrieve. Not every query needs RAG.

questions.scenario: A team wants the most robust production RAG retrieval configuration. They need good precision without excessive complexity.
questions.question: What is the recommended production default for RAG retrieval?

options.text: Pure vector search for simplicity
Hierarchical chunking + hybrid search + reranking
Keyword search only
Sentence-level chunking with exact match

questions.explanation: The production pattern is: hierarchical chunking + hybrid search + reranking. This is the most robust default—hierarchical preserves context, hybrid catches both semantic and keyword matches, reranking improves precision.

incorrectExplanations.a: Pure vector search misses exact keyword matches.
incorrectExplanations.c: Keyword-only misses semantic similarity.
incorrectExplanations.d: Sentence-level is too granular for most use cases.

questions.articleReference: Retrieval Mechanisms > Production Pattern: 'Hierarchical chunking + Hybrid search + Reranking. This is the most robust default for production RAG.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Production RAG = hierarchical + hybrid + reranking.

questions.examTip: Production RAG default: hierarchical chunking + hybrid search + reranking.

questions.scenario: A team wants their customer service AI to respond in a specific format with empathetic tone. Describing the format in words isn't producing consistent results.
questions.question: Which prompting technique is MOST effective when format is hard to describe in words?

options.text: Chain-of-thought reasoning
Role prompting only
Few-shot with 2-5 examples demonstrating the desired format
Zero-shot with detailed instructions

questions.explanation: Few-shot prompting shows 2-5 examples before the actual request. Instead of explaining how you want responses formatted, demonstrate it. Include both positive AND negative examples to show what NOT to do.

incorrectExplanations.a: Chain-of-thought is for reasoning accuracy, not format consistency.
incorrectExplanations.b: Role prompting sets persona but doesn't demonstrate format.
incorrectExplanations.d: Zero-shot relies on description, which the scenario says isn't working.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Few-Shot: 'Show 2-5 examples before your actual request. Instead of explaining exactly how you want responses formatted, demonstrate it. Few-shot is powerful when format or style is hard to describe in words.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Format consistency = few-shot examples.

questions.examTip: Few-shot when format is hard to describe. Show examples, include negatives.

questions.scenario: A math tutoring AI frequently makes calculation errors on multi-step word problems. The team wants to improve accuracy without changing the model.
questions.question: Which prompting technique can DOUBLE accuracy on reasoning tasks?

options.text: Chain-of-thought: 'Let's think through this step by step'
JSON output format
Higher temperature for creativity
Shorter prompts for faster processing

questions.explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting asks the model to reason step-by-step. Just adding 'Let's think through this step by step' often doubles accuracy on math, logic, and multi-step analysis. The reasoning chain also aids debugging.

incorrectExplanations.b: JSON format is for output structure, not reasoning accuracy.
incorrectExplanations.c: Higher temperature increases randomness, potentially reducing accuracy.
incorrectExplanations.d: Shorter prompts don't improve reasoning accuracy.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Chain-of-Thought: 'Just adding "Let's think through this step by step" before asking for an answer often doubles accuracy.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Reasoning/math errors = chain-of-thought prompting.

questions.examTip: Chain-of-thought doubles accuracy on reasoning tasks. 'Think step by step.'

questions.scenario: Multiple teams in an organization are writing prompts independently. Quality varies wildly, best practices don't propagate, and there's no version history when prompts change.
questions.question: Which AWS service addresses these prompt governance challenges?

options.text: AWS CodeCommit
Amazon S3 with versioning
Bedrock Prompt Management
AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store

questions.explanation: Bedrock Prompt Management is the single source of truth for prompts. Teams reference shared prompts rather than copying them. Every change is tracked—who modified what, when. When a prompt improves, everyone benefits immediately.

incorrectExplanations.a: CodeCommit is for code, not prompt lifecycle management.
incorrectExplanations.b: S3 can store prompts but lacks prompt-specific features like testing and guardrail integration.
incorrectExplanations.d: Parameter Store is for configuration values, not prompt management.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Bedrock Prompt Management: 'Your single source of truth. Store prompts centrally with version control. Every change is tracked—who modified what, when.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Prompt governance/versioning = Bedrock Prompt Management.

questions.examTip: Bedrock Prompt Management for centralized, versioned prompt governance.

questions.scenario: A document processing pipeline requires: (1) summarize, (2) extract entities from summary, (3) generate recommendations. Each step uses a different prompt focus.
questions.question: Which orchestration approach is recommended for this multi-stage prompt pipeline?

options.text: User selection of which prompt to run
Parallel execution of all three prompts
Prompt chaining with separate focused prompts for each stage
Single monolithic prompt that does all three steps

questions.explanation: Prompt chaining breaks complex tasks into stages, each using a focused prompt. This often beats a monolithic prompt trying to do everything at once. Each stage can be optimized independently.

incorrectExplanations.a: User selection defeats the purpose of an automated pipeline.
incorrectExplanations.b: These steps are sequential (extract FROM summary), not parallel.
incorrectExplanations.d: Monolithic prompts trying to do everything often perform worse than chains.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Prompt Flows and Chaining: 'Each stage uses a focused prompt. This often beats a monolithic prompt trying to do everything at once.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Multi-stage processing = prompt chaining.

questions.examTip: Prompt chaining for multi-stage tasks. Focused prompts beat monolithic ones.

questions.scenario: An AI application returns well-formed JSON most of the time, but occasionally produces malformed output that crashes downstream systems.
questions.question: What output quality practice should be implemented?

options.text: Parse JSON output and validate schema before passing downstream
Use a smaller, faster model
Trust the model to always produce valid JSON
Increase temperature for more creative output

questions.explanation: FM outputs are probabilistic—the same prompt can produce different outputs. If you requested JSON, parse it and verify the schema. Check for missing fields and invalid values. Either retry with clarification or return an error. Don't pass malformed data downstream.

incorrectExplanations.b: Model size doesn't guarantee JSON validity.
incorrectExplanations.c: Never trust FM output without validation.
incorrectExplanations.d: Higher temperature increases output variability, making this worse.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Structured Output Validation: 'If you requested JSON, parse it and verify the schema. Missing fields? Invalid values? Either retry with clarification or return an error. Don't pass malformed data downstream.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Structured output = validate before using downstream.

questions.examTip: Always validate FM output. Parse JSON, check schema, handle malformed data.

questions.scenario: A chatbot conversation has grown very long and is approaching model context limits. The team needs to continue the conversation without losing important earlier context.
questions.question: What technique should be used when conversation history exceeds model limits?

options.text: Switch to a model with unlimited context
Truncate from the beginning, keeping only recent messages
Summarize older turns while keeping recent turns intact
Start a new conversation and lose all history

questions.explanation: When conversation history exceeds model limits, summarize older turns while keeping recent turns intact. This preserves important context while staying within limits. Alternatives include dropping oldest turns or selective retrieval of relevant history.

incorrectExplanations.a: No model has unlimited context.
incorrectExplanations.b: Simple truncation loses important context from earlier in the conversation.
incorrectExplanations.d: Losing all history is a poor user experience.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Context Window Limits: 'When conversation history exceeds model limits: Summarize older turns, Drop the oldest, or Selective retrieval of relevant history.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Long conversations = summarize older turns.

questions.examTip: Context limit exceeded: summarize older turns. Preserve meaning, reduce tokens.

questions.scenario: A team is considering fine-tuning their model because output formatting is inconsistent. They haven't tried other approaches yet.
questions.question: According to prompt engineering best practices, what should they try FIRST?

options.text: Switch to a larger model
Fine-tune immediately for best results
Exhaust prompt engineering options—it's free to iterate
Use continued pre-training

questions.explanation: Before fine-tuning, exhaust prompt options. Fine-tuning costs time and money (takes weeks, costs thousands). Prompts are free to iterate. Many 'I need to fine-tune' situations are actually 'I need better prompts.'

incorrectExplanations.a: Larger models cost more and don't guarantee format consistency.
incorrectExplanations.b: Fine-tuning should be a last resort after prompt engineering.
incorrectExplanations.d: Continued pre-training adds knowledge, not formatting behavior.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Common Mistakes: 'Fine-tuning before exhausting prompt options: Fine-tuning costs time and money. Prompt engineering is free to iterate and often solves the problem.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Always try prompts before fine-tuning.

questions.examTip: Prompt engineering first, always. Free to iterate. Fine-tuning = weeks + thousands.

questions.scenario: A prompt flow needs sophisticated branching, parallel execution, error handling with retries, and deep AWS integration. Visual no-code tools aren't flexible enough.
questions.question: Which orchestration service should be used for complex prompt flows?

options.text: Bedrock Prompt Flows
AWS Step Functions
AWS Lambda only
Amazon EventBridge

questions.explanation: When you need sophisticated branching, parallel execution, error handling with retries, or deep AWS integration, Step Functions delivers the control that visual tools can't match. Prompt Flows is better for simple to moderate chains.

incorrectExplanations.a: Prompt Flows is for simple to moderate chains, not complex orchestration.
incorrectExplanations.c: Lambda alone requires you to implement all orchestration logic.
incorrectExplanations.d: EventBridge is for event routing, not workflow orchestration.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Orchestration Comparison: 'Step Functions: Complex branching, parallel, error handling... more power for complex logic.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Complex orchestration needs = Step Functions.

questions.examTip: Prompt Flows = simple chains. Step Functions = complex orchestration.

questions.scenario: A regulated financial services company needs to track who accessed or modified prompts, when changes were made, and maintain an immutable audit trail for compliance.
questions.question: Which combination of services provides prompt audit trails for compliance?

options.text: S3 with versioning and object lock + CloudTrail
DynamoDB with point-in-time recovery
CodeCommit with branch protection
Bedrock Prompt Management only

questions.explanation: S3 with versioning and object lock provides an immutable audit trail for prompt templates. CloudTrail logs all prompt access and modifications: who accessed, what changed, when. Essential for regulated industries.

incorrectExplanations.b: DynamoDB isn't designed for prompt template storage and auditing.
incorrectExplanations.c: CodeCommit is for code, not prompt governance with compliance requirements.
incorrectExplanations.d: Prompt Management provides versioning but CloudTrail adds the detailed access logging.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Audit Trail: 'CloudTrail logs prompt access and modifications for compliance. S3 with versioning and object lock provides an immutable audit trail.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Compliance audit trail = CloudTrail + S3 versioning/object lock.

questions.examTip: Compliance audit trails: CloudTrail for access logs, S3 object lock for immutability.

questions.scenario: A model occasionally produces suspicious output. The team wants the model to review its own response before delivery to catch potential errors.
questions.question: Which advanced prompting technique asks the model to review its own output?

options.text: Role prompting
Self-critique prompting
Zero-shot prompting
Few-shot prompting

questions.explanation: Self-critique asks the model to review its own output: 'Review your response for accuracy and completeness. Are there any errors or omissions?' The critique often catches issues the initial generation missed.

incorrectExplanations.a: Role prompting sets persona but doesn't include self-review.
incorrectExplanations.c: Zero-shot gives instructions without examples or self-review.
incorrectExplanations.d: Few-shot provides examples but doesn't include self-review.

questions.articleReference: Prompt Engineering > Self-Critique: 'Ask the model to review its own output: "Review your response for accuracy and completeness. Are there any errors?" The critique often catches issues the initial generation missed.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Model self-review = self-critique prompting.

questions.examTip: Self-critique: have model review its own output. Catches errors in initial generation.

questions.scenario: A foundation model is asked about the company's 2024 vacation policy. It confidently provides an answer that sounds reasonable but is completely fabricated.
questions.question: What is this phenomenon called, and what pattern prevents it?

options.text: Bias; use balanced training data
Overfitting; use regularization
Underfitting; use a larger model
Hallucination; use RAG to ground responses in actual documents

questions.explanation: When FMs don't know something, they don't say 'I don't know'—they make something up. This is called hallucination. RAG solves this by grounding responses in actual retrieved documents, so the FM synthesizes answers from real sources.

incorrectExplanations.a: Bias is about systematic errors, not fabricated facts.
incorrectExplanations.b: Overfitting is a training issue, not a generation issue.
incorrectExplanations.c: Larger models can hallucinate more convincingly, not less.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > The Fundamental Problem: 'When you ask an FM something it doesn't know, it doesn't say "I don't know." It makes something up. This is called hallucination, and it's the core problem RAG solves.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Fabricated answers = hallucination. Fix with RAG.

questions.examTip: Hallucination = FM making up answers. RAG grounds responses in actual documents.

questions.scenario: A team is deciding between RAG and fine-tuning to add company-specific knowledge to their AI. They need to update this knowledge frequently as policies change, and they want users to see cited sources.
questions.question: Which approach is MOST appropriate for this use case?

options.text: Continued pre-training for domain knowledge
RAG, because knowledge updates are quick and sources can be cited
Fine-tuning, because it bakes knowledge into model weights
Either approach works equally well

questions.explanation: RAG keeps knowledge external and retrievable. Updating knowledge means re-indexing documents (hours), not re-training the model (days, expensive). RAG can cite sources because it knows which documents were retrieved. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into weights—no citations possible.

incorrectExplanations.a: Pre-training adds knowledge but doesn't enable citations or quick updates.
incorrectExplanations.c: Fine-tuning requires retraining for updates and can't cite sources.
incorrectExplanations.d: They're not equal—RAG is clearly better for frequent updates and citations.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Why Not Just Fine-Tune: 'RAG: Updating knowledge = re-index docs (hours). Fine-tuning: Re-train the model (days, expensive). RAG: Can cite sources. Fine-tuning: Knowledge buried in weights.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Frequent updates + source citations = RAG over fine-tuning.

questions.examTip: RAG for dynamic knowledge + citations. Fine-tuning for style/behavior changes.

questions.scenario: A RAG system produces wrong answers. The team needs to debug. The retrieved chunks look relevant, and the answer seems to be in the chunks, but the FM's response is incorrect.
questions.question: According to the RAG debugging workflow, which link in the quality chain has likely failed?

options.text: Retrieval—wrong documents were found
Embedding—vectors are incorrect
Generation—FM ignored or misused the context
Chunking—documents were split incorrectly

questions.explanation: The debugging workflow: 1) Check retrieval—right docs found? YES. 2) Check context—answer in docs? YES. 3) Check generation—FM used context correctly? NO. This is a prompt engineering issue—the FM isn't properly using the provided context.

incorrectExplanations.a: The scenario says chunks look relevant—retrieval worked.
incorrectExplanations.b: Embedding issues would cause retrieval problems.
incorrectExplanations.d: Chunking issues would cause retrieval problems.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Debugging Workflow: '1. Check retrieval → Were the right docs found? Yes → Continue. 2. Check context → Is the answer in the retrieved docs? Yes → Continue. 3. Check generation → Did the FM use the context correctly? No → Prompt engineering issue.'

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: retrieved chunks look relevant, answer is in chunks, response incorrect
eliminationHints: Good retrieval, good context, bad output = generation issue
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: RAG debugging: retrieval → context → generation.

questions.examTip: RAG debugging: check retrieval first, then context quality, then generation.

questions.scenario: In a multi-turn chatbot, a user asks 'What about the pricing?' after discussing Lambda. The RAG system retrieves documents about general 'pricing' topics instead of Lambda pricing.
questions.question: What RAG pattern addresses this context-dependent retrieval issue?

options.text: Conversational RAG that reformulates queries with conversation context
Basic RAG with higher retrieval count
Agentic RAG with tool calling
Multi-index RAG with routing

questions.explanation: Conversational RAG incorporates conversation history into the search. 'What about the pricing?' is reformulated to a standalone question like 'What is AWS Lambda pricing?' before retrieval. This resolves pronouns and context references.

incorrectExplanations.b: More results doesn't fix the context-dependent query issue.
incorrectExplanations.c: Agentic RAG is for complex multi-step retrieval, not context resolution.
incorrectExplanations.d: Multi-index routing doesn't resolve conversational context.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Conversational RAG: 'Incorporate conversation history into the search. "What about the pricing?" means nothing without knowing what "that" refers to from earlier.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Conversational context issues = Conversational RAG.

questions.examTip: Conversational RAG reformulates queries using conversation history.

questions.scenario: A large enterprise has separate document repositories for HR policies, technical documentation, and legal contracts. User queries should only search the relevant repository.
questions.question: Which RAG pattern routes queries to specialized knowledge bases?

options.text: Agentic RAG
Multi-Index RAG with query routing
Basic RAG with metadata filtering
Conversational RAG

questions.explanation: Multi-Index RAG routes queries to specialized knowledge bases based on query content or user context. If query mentions 'policy', route to POLICY_KB. If 'technical', route to TECHNICAL_KB. Each domain has its own optimized index.

incorrectExplanations.a: Agentic RAG is for complex reasoning, not simple routing.
incorrectExplanations.c: Metadata filtering works within one index, not across separate knowledge bases.
incorrectExplanations.d: Conversational RAG is for context resolution, not routing.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Multi-Index RAG: 'Route queries to specialized knowledge bases. If "policy" in query → POLICY_KB. If "technical" → TECHNICAL_KB.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Multiple specialized KBs = Multi-Index RAG with routing.

questions.examTip: Multi-Index RAG routes queries to specialized knowledge bases by topic.

questions.scenario: A complex research question requires the AI to: (1) search for initial context, (2) reason about gaps, (3) search again for additional information, (4) synthesize a final answer.
questions.question: Which RAG pattern supports this iterative search-reason-search behavior?

options.text: Agentic RAG where the agent decides when and what to search
Conversational RAG with history
Basic RAG with RetrieveAndGenerate
Multi-Index RAG with routing

questions.explanation: Agentic RAG lets the agent reason and decide when to search. The pattern is: Query → Agent reasons → Decides to search → Retrieves → Reasons more → Maybe searches again → Response. Best for complex questions requiring multiple lookups.

incorrectExplanations.b: Conversational RAG handles history, not iterative retrieval.
incorrectExplanations.c: Basic RAG does one retrieval, not iterative search-reason-search.
incorrectExplanations.d: Multi-Index RAG routes once, doesn't iterate.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Agentic RAG: 'Query → Agent reasons → Decides to search → Retrieves → Reasons more → Maybe searches again → Response. Best for: Complex questions requiring multiple lookups.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Iterative retrieval needs = Agentic RAG.

questions.examTip: Agentic RAG: agent decides when/what to search. For complex multi-step queries.

questions.scenario: A team wants to implement RAG with minimal code. They want AWS to handle retrieval and generation in a single API call.
questions.question: Which Bedrock Knowledge Bases API provides this all-in-one RAG capability?

options.text: RetrieveAndGenerate API
Retrieve API
Query API
InvokeModel API

questions.explanation: RetrieveAndGenerate does full RAG in one call: retrieves relevant documents from the knowledge base and generates a response using an FM. Use this for simple RAG. Use Retrieve alone when you need custom prompts or non-Bedrock FMs.

incorrectExplanations.b: Retrieve only returns documents—you handle generation yourself.
incorrectExplanations.c: Query API doesn't exist for Knowledge Bases.
incorrectExplanations.d: InvokeModel calls models directly without retrieval.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > RetrieveAndGenerate: 'Full RAG in one call. Use for simple RAG. Use Retrieve alone when you need custom prompts or non-Bedrock FMs.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Simple RAG = RetrieveAndGenerate. Custom = Retrieve + custom generation.

questions.examTip: RetrieveAndGenerate for simple RAG. Retrieve alone for custom processing.

questions.scenario: A RAG system's quality seems to have degraded after recent prompt changes. The team has no systematic way to detect this.
questions.question: What evaluation practice would catch quality regression?

options.text: Manual spot-checking of random queries
Token usage monitoring
Golden dataset testing with known query-answer pairs
User satisfaction surveys

questions.explanation: Maintain test cases with known answers (golden dataset). Run against these after any change to chunking, embeddings, retrieval, or prompts. Assert quality scores remain above threshold. This catches regression before users notice.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual spot-checking doesn't scale and misses systematic issues.
incorrectExplanations.b: Token usage doesn't indicate answer quality.
incorrectExplanations.d: Surveys are lagging indicators—users already experienced problems.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Golden Dataset Testing: 'Maintain test cases with known answers. Run regression tests after any change to catch quality degradation before users do.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Quality regression detection = golden dataset testing.

questions.examTip: Golden dataset = test cases with known answers. Run after every change.

questions.scenario: A team measures retrieval quality. They want to know: 'Of all the relevant chunks that exist in our corpus, how many did we actually retrieve?'
questions.question: Which metric answers this question?

options.text: F1 score
Latency
Recall
Precision

questions.explanation: Recall measures: of all the relevant chunks that exist, how many did you retrieve? Precision measures: of the chunks you retrieved, how many were actually relevant? There's a tradeoff—retrieve more for higher recall, fewer for higher precision.

incorrectExplanations.a: F1 is a balance of precision and recall, not this specific question.
incorrectExplanations.b: Latency measures speed, not retrieval completeness.
incorrectExplanations.d: Precision measures relevance of retrieved results, not coverage of all relevant results.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > The Two Metrics: 'Recall: Of all the relevant chunks that exist, how many did you retrieve? Precision: Of the chunks you retrieved, how many were actually relevant?'

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: of all relevant, how many retrieved
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Coverage of relevant docs = recall. Relevance of retrieved = precision.

questions.examTip: Recall = did we find all relevant docs? Precision = are retrieved docs relevant?

questions.scenario: A RAG system returns 'I don't know' for a question when the answer definitely exists in the indexed documents. The team suspects a retrieval issue.
questions.question: What should they check FIRST in the debugging workflow?

options.text: Check if the document is indexed and similarity score isn't too low
Check if the model is hallucinating
Check if the FM prompt is correct
Check if the output guardrails are blocking the response

questions.explanation: For 'I don't know' when the answer exists: First check retrieval. Is the document indexed? Is the similarity score too low (adjust threshold)? Is the answer split across chunk boundaries? Does the query language match the document language?

incorrectExplanations.b: Hallucination produces wrong answers, not 'I don't know'.
incorrectExplanations.c: Prompt issues cause wrong answers, not 'I don't know' when docs exist.
incorrectExplanations.d: Guardrails blocking would show intervention message, not 'I don't know'.

questions.articleReference: RAG Patterns > Debugging: 'Problem: "I don't know" when the answer exists. Is the document indexed? Is similarity score too low? Is the answer split across chunk boundaries? Does query language match document language?'

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: I don't know when answer exists
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Missing answers = check retrieval first (indexed? threshold? boundaries?).

questions.examTip: 'I don't know' errors: check if doc indexed, similarity threshold, chunk boundaries.

questions.scenario: A developer is trying to run foundation models on their EC2 instances using Bedrock. They're confused about where the model actually runs.
questions.question: How does Amazon Bedrock host foundation models?

options.text: Models are deployed to the customer's containers
Bedrock provides a managed API layer—AWS hosts all model infrastructure
Models are deployed to the customer's EC2 instances
Customers download models and run them locally

questions.explanation: Bedrock is a fully managed service that provides API access to foundation models. It is NOT hosting models in your account. AWS handles scaling, availability, and updates. You just make API calls.

incorrectExplanations.a: Models don't run in customer containers.
incorrectExplanations.c: Models don't run on customer EC2 instances.
incorrectExplanations.d: Models aren't downloaded—they're accessed via API.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > How Bedrock Actually Works: 'Bedrock is not hosting models in your account. It provides a managed API layer that routes your requests to model infrastructure AWS manages.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Bedrock = managed API. AWS hosts models, you make API calls.

questions.examTip: Bedrock is a managed API layer. No infrastructure in your account.

questions.scenario: A chatbot using InvokeModel takes 10-30 seconds to respond to complex queries. Users wait with a loading spinner seeing nothing until the full response is ready.
questions.question: Which Bedrock API should replace InvokeModel to improve perceived latency?

options.text: InvokeModelWithResponseStream
Converse API
BatchInference API
ApplyGuardrail API

questions.explanation: InvokeModelWithResponseStream returns tokens as they're generated instead of waiting for the complete response. First tokens arrive in ~500ms, and text builds progressively. Total time is unchanged, but perceived latency drops dramatically.

incorrectExplanations.b: Converse API doesn't inherently stream unless you use converseStream.
incorrectExplanations.c: Batch inference is for large-scale async processing, not real-time chatbots.
incorrectExplanations.d: ApplyGuardrail is for content filtering, not response delivery.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > InvokeModelWithResponseStream: 'Tokens arrive as generated. Perceived latency: Much better (first token in ~500ms). Total time: Same as sync, but user sees progress.'

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: loading spinner, waiting for full response
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Long wait with spinner = switch to streaming.

questions.examTip: InvokeModelWithResponseStream for chatbots. First token in ~500ms.

questions.scenario: A team is calling different Bedrock models (Claude, Titan, Llama) and managing different payload formats for each. They want a unified API format across all models.
questions.question: Which Bedrock API provides a unified format across model families?

options.text: InvokeModel with model-specific payloads
Custom wrapper functions
Converse API with standardized message format
BatchInference API

questions.explanation: The Converse API provides a unified format across model families. It handles conversation history with a standardized structure. Easier than managing model-specific payload formats for each provider.

incorrectExplanations.a: InvokeModel requires different payload formats per model family.
incorrectExplanations.b: Custom wrappers add complexity and must be maintained.
incorrectExplanations.d: BatchInference is for async processing, not API unification.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Converse API: 'Unified format across model families. Handles conversation history. Easier than model-specific payload formats.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Unified API across models = Converse API.

questions.examTip: Converse API unifies formats across Claude, Titan, Llama, etc.

questions.scenario: A production application needs to block harmful content, prevent discussion of medical diagnosis, and protect PII in both inputs and outputs.
questions.question: Which Bedrock Guardrails components address each of these requirements?

options.text: Word filters for all three requirements
Content filters for harmful content; denied topics for medical; PII filters for sensitive data
Content filters only—they handle everything
Denied topics for all three requirements

questions.explanation: Guardrails have multiple policy types: Content filters block harmful content (hate, violence, sexual). Denied topics block specific conversation topics (like medical diagnosis). PII filters detect and handle sensitive data (BLOCK or ANONYMIZE actions).

incorrectExplanations.a: Word filters are for specific words/phrases, not semantic categories.
incorrectExplanations.c: Content filters handle harmful categories but not topic blocking or PII.
incorrectExplanations.d: Denied topics are for conversation topics, not content categories or PII.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Guardrail Components: 'Content Filters: Block harmful content. Denied Topics: Block specific conversation topics. PII Detection: Identify and handle personal information. Actions: BLOCK or ANONYMIZE.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Harmful content = content filters. Topics = denied topics. PII = PII filters.

questions.examTip: Know guardrail types: content filters, denied topics, word filters, PII filters.

questions.scenario: A team wants to check user input against guardrails BEFORE sending it to the model, to save costs on rejected requests.
questions.question: Which API allows applying guardrails without invoking a model?

options.text: No such capability exists
Converse API with guardrails enabled
ApplyGuardrail API with source='INPUT'
InvokeModel with guardrailIdentifier

questions.explanation: ApplyGuardrail API applies guardrails without invoking a model. Set source='INPUT' to check user input before sending to the model. Set source='OUTPUT' to check model responses. Useful for pre-checking to save costs.

incorrectExplanations.a: The capability exists—ApplyGuardrail API.
incorrectExplanations.b: Converse with guardrails still invokes the model.
incorrectExplanations.d: InvokeModel applies guardrails during invocation, using model tokens.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > ApplyGuardrail API: 'Apply guardrails without invoking a model. source='INPUT' or source='OUTPUT'. Useful for pre-checking user input.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Pre-check without model = ApplyGuardrail API.

questions.examTip: ApplyGuardrail API: check input/output without invoking model.

questions.scenario: A startup wants the cheapest model for simple FAQ responses. A financial firm needs the most capable model for complex document analysis.
questions.question: Which Claude 3 models should each organization choose?

options.text: Both should use Claude 3 Opus for best quality
Startup: Claude 3 Haiku (cheapest/fastest). Financial firm: Claude 3 Opus (most capable)
Both should use Claude 3 Sonnet for balance
Startup: Titan Text. Financial firm: Claude 3 Haiku

questions.explanation: Claude 3 Haiku is cheapest ($0.25/1M input tokens) and fastest—perfect for simple tasks. Claude 3 Opus is most capable ($15/1M input tokens) for complex reasoning. The 60x price difference means matching model to task matters.

incorrectExplanations.a: Opus for simple FAQ is wasteful—60x more expensive than needed.
incorrectExplanations.c: Sonnet is balanced but not optimal for either extreme use case.
incorrectExplanations.d: This reverses the appropriate mapping.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Model Families: 'Claude 3 Haiku: Fastest, cheapest, simple tasks. Claude 3 Opus: Most capable, complex reasoning.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Simple tasks = Haiku. Complex reasoning = Opus.

questions.examTip: Haiku: $0.25/1M (cheapest). Sonnet: $3/1M. Opus: $15/1M (most capable).

questions.scenario: A high-volume application wants to reduce per-token costs but can commit to sustained usage for the next 6 months.
questions.question: Which Bedrock pricing option should they evaluate?

options.text: On-demand pricing for flexibility
Provisioned Throughput with commitment
Batch inference
Free tier usage

questions.explanation: Provisioned Throughput offers lower effective per-token costs at high utilization. Purchase Model Units with 1-month or 6-month commitments. Break-even is typically around 40-60% utilization. Six-month commitments offer better rates.

incorrectExplanations.a: On-demand is more expensive at high volumes.
incorrectExplanations.c: Batch inference is for async workloads, not real-time high-volume.
incorrectExplanations.d: There's no free tier for high-volume production usage.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Provisioned Throughput: 'Lower effective per-token cost at high utilization. 1-month or 6-month commitments. Break-even typically around 40-60% utilization.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Sustained high volume = consider Provisioned Throughput.

questions.examTip: Provisioned Throughput: commit capacity for lower per-token costs. ~40-60% break-even.

questions.scenario: A Bedrock Agent is producing unexpected responses. The team can't understand why it's making certain tool calls or skipping others.
questions.question: What feature should they enable to understand the agent's reasoning?

options.text: Agent tracing
X-Ray tracing
CloudWatch Logs
VPC Flow Logs

questions.explanation: Agent tracing shows the agent's reasoning process: which tools it considered, which it called, what results it received, and how it formulated the response. Essential for debugging and understanding agent behavior.

incorrectExplanations.b: X-Ray traces request flow, not agent reasoning.
incorrectExplanations.c: CloudWatch Logs show invocations but not reasoning process.
incorrectExplanations.d: VPC Flow Logs show network traffic, not agent decisions.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Agent Tracing: 'Enable tracing to see the agent's reasoning: Which tools it considered, which tools it called, what results it received, how it formulated the response.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Agent debugging = enable agent tracing.

questions.examTip: Agent tracing shows reasoning: tools considered, called, results, response formation.

questions.scenario: A Bedrock Agent needs to get current weather data. The team needs to define what the agent can do and how it executes those actions.
questions.question: What two components define an agent's action capabilities?

options.text: Prompt template and guardrails
IAM role and VPC configuration
OpenAPI schema defining the action + Lambda function implementing it
Knowledge base and embedding model

questions.explanation: Action Groups define agent capabilities with two parts: an OpenAPI schema describing the API (parameters, types, descriptions) and a Lambda function that executes the actual logic. The agent uses the schema to know what's available and calls Lambda to execute.

incorrectExplanations.a: Prompts and guardrails are agent components but not action definitions.
incorrectExplanations.b: IAM and VPC are security/networking, not action definition.
incorrectExplanations.d: Knowledge bases are for retrieval, not action execution.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Action Groups: 'Each action is backed by a Lambda function. OpenAPI Schema defines the API. Lambda function implements the logic.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Agent actions = OpenAPI schema + Lambda function.

questions.examTip: Action Groups: OpenAPI schema (what) + Lambda function (how).

questions.scenario: A team is building a cost optimization strategy. They want to route simple classification requests to the cheapest model while complex analysis goes to a more capable model.
questions.question: What pattern implements this cost optimization approach?

options.text: Model cascading—classify first, then route to appropriate model
Batch inference for all requests
Provisioned Throughput for all models
Always use the most capable model

questions.explanation: Model cascading routes requests to the appropriate model based on complexity. A cheap classifier determines if the request is simple (send to Haiku) or complex (send to Sonnet/Opus). 80% or more of requests can often use the cheapest model.

incorrectExplanations.b: Batch inference is for latency tolerance, not routing decisions.
incorrectExplanations.c: Provisioned Throughput doesn't implement routing logic.
incorrectExplanations.d: Using the most capable model for everything wastes money on simple tasks.

questions.articleReference: Bedrock Deep Dive > Model Cascading: 'Route every request first to a fast, cheap model. If confident, use that response. If uncertain, escalate. 80% or more of requests can be handled by the cheapest model.'

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Cost optimization through routing = model cascading.

questions.examTip: Model cascading: classify → route simple to cheap model, complex to capable model.
